text: Anti-gravity treadmill use reduces cadence, ground reaction forces, GRF impulses, knee and ankle range of motion, and vertical stiffness compared to full body weight running.
Anti-gravity treadmill use increases stride duration, flight time, ground contact time, and plantarflexion compared to full body weight running.
Runners using anti-gravity treadmills may preserve aerobic fitness, muscle activation patterns, and muscle mass during recovery compared to traditional rehabilitation protocols.

Anti-gravity treadmills appear useful for rehabilitation across a spectrum of running injuries including postsurgical osteochondral defect repair, stress reactions (medial tibia, pelvis), and lumbar disc herniation.
Fast cadence on anti-gravity treadmills should be monitored because it can increase impact loading rates despite body weight support.
Speed or grade on anti-gravity treadmills can be increased to maintain metabolic demand and aerobic fitness while minimizing bone and tissue loading during rehabilitation.

A runner who can run pain-free (sustained or interval) at greater than 95% body weight support for more than 30 minutes on an anti-gravity treadmill is likely ready to safely transition to ground running.
